coredata.dc:title: Functional graphene carbon dots in corn stalk pith as an efficient sorbent for preconcentration and trace determination of triazole residues in rice samples
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Silaram, Woranut
Boonlerd, Rossukhon
Rattanaphonsaen, Phatchara
Khiaophong, Wannipha
Kachangoon, Rawikan
Teshima, Norio
Pakkethati, Kansiri
Mukdasai, Siriboon
Ponhong, Kraingkrai
Vichapong, Jitlada
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a simpler synthesis process by using environmentally friendly reagents for obtaining functional graphene carbon dots in corn stalk pith as a green sorbent is presented. The as-prepared sorbent used for micro-solid phase extraction and preconcentration of triazole fungicides in rice samples followed by HPLC. Moreover, the characterization of as-prepared sorbent was investigated. Key parameters affecting the processes of adsorption and desorption process were optimized. For the preparation of corn stalk pith, 0.1 g of citric acid, 0.25 g of urea and 0.1 g of graphite powder and heated in the oven for 150 min at 130 °C to fully modify. After that, 400 µL of methanol was used for desorbed the analytes. Under the optimum condition, good analytical figures of merit through wide liner range (0.09–1.0 µg L−1) with determination coefficients (R2) higher than 0.98, were achieved with an enrichment factor of up to 12 were obtained. The LODs and LOQs were 0.01–0.03 μg L−1 and 0.03–0.09 μg L−1, respectively. They have been successfully applied to the analysis of rice samples. The recoveries in a satisfactory range of 59.46–139.20 % were obtained, indicating a low matrix effect and the robustness of the optimized method. This alternative sorbent was shown good selectivity and cost-effective, due to their low cost biosorbents.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fungicides
Carbon dots
Graphene
Corn stalk pith
Sample preconcentration
Biosorbent

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of corn stalk pith and functional graphene carbon dots in corn stalk pith as a green sorbent
Micro-solid phase extraction (micro-SPE) using green investigated sorbent
Characterization of the corn stalk pith decorated with graphene carbon dots
The adsorption mechanism of graphene carbon dots in corn stalk pith
Analytical figures of merit of the studied method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of (a) urea, (b) citric acid, (c) graphite, (d) corn stalk pith, (e) corn stalk pith decorated with graphene carbon dots and (f) corn stalk pith decorated with graphene carbon dots after adsorption of triazole fungicides (100 µg L−1).

SEM images of (a) corn stalk pith, (b) corn stalk pith decorated with graphene carbon dots, (c) corn stalk pith decorated with graphene carbon dots after adsorption of triazole fungicides (100 µg L−1) and (d) corn stalk pith decorated with graphene carbon dots after elution step.
XRD pattern of (a) graphite, (b) corn stalk pith and (c) corn stalk pith decorated with graphene carbon dots.
TGA curves of (a) corn stalk pith and (b) corn stalk pith decorated with graphene carbon dots.
BET surface area, pore size and pore volume of corn stalk pith.
Analytical characteristics of proposed method for standard triazoles.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Precision were calculated at the concentration of 100 μg L−1 of each triazole.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Ratio of the slope of regression equations with and without the application of the proposed method.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analytical Eco-Scale (AEC) and Analytical greenness metric (AGREE).
Comparison of extraction methods for determination of triazole fungicides.

row.entry: Environmental water, honey and fruit juice samples
Corn stalk pith decorated with graphene carbon dots

head.ce:title: Functional graphene carbon dots in corn stalk pith as an efficient sorbent for preconcentration and trace determination of triazole residues in rice samples

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Creative Chemistry and Innovation Research Unit

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a simpler synthesis process by using environmentally friendly reagents for obtaining functional graphene carbon dots in corn stalk pith as a green sorbent is presented. The as-prepared sorbent used for micro-solid phase extraction and preconcentration of triazole fungicides in rice samples followed by HPLC. Moreover, the characterization of as-prepared sorbent was investigated. Key parameters affecting the processes of adsorption and desorption process were optimized. For the preparation of corn stalk pith, 0.1 g of citric acid, 0.25 g of urea and 0.1 g of graphite powder and heated in the oven for 150 min at 130 °C to fully modify. After that, 400 µL of methanol was used for desorbed the analytes. Under the optimum condition, good analytical figures of merit through wide liner range (0.09–1.0 µg L−1) with determination coefficients (R2) higher than 0.98, were achieved with an enrichment factor of up to 12 were obtained. The LODs and LOQs were 0.01–0.03 μg L−1 and 0.03–0.09 μg L−1, respectively. They have been successfully applied to the analysis of rice samples. The recoveries in a satisfactory range of 59.46–139.20 % were obtained, indicating a low matrix effect and the robustness of the optimized method. This alternative sorbent was shown good selectivity and cost-effective, due to their low cost biosorbents.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Rice (Oryza sativa L.) is the most staple crop in the world either directly as human foods or indirectly as animal feeds [1]. It is the most staple food in Thailand. Rice often suffer from bacterial and fungal diseases in the production process. Therefore, people often spray some antibacterial agents regularly to kill bacteria and antifungals after food harvest to prevent the impact of diseases and improve the yield [2]. Triazole fungicides are the most attractive group of high-performance, against almost all fungal diseases [3]. Sensitive and selective analytical techniques for the quantification of triazole fungicides to ensure that the residue levels under safety band of the maximum residue limits (MRLs) would require.

Due to the complexity involved in the simultaneous analysis of triazole fungicides, sample pretreatment is one of the key steps [4]. Solid-phase extraction (SPE) [5,6] has been widely utilized in the sample preparation process, which is not only preparation but also enrichment of the target compounds on a solid adsorbent through adsorption from the solution [7] prior to their analysis. The interaction such as electrostatic interaction between the target analyte and the sorbent was occurred after loading, and then a small amount of an optimal solvent was used to elute the analytes from the sorbent [8]. Recently, there has been high prioritization and utilization of green chemistry principles due to the increasing need for using fewer organic solvents and short times [9]. Nowadays, attention is being paid to the development of simple, effective and miniaturized sample preparation method [10], such as micro-SPE, is acquiring a lot of attention due to the impressive decrease in the amounts of sorbents [11] and little amount of organic solvents required [12]. The adsorbents used in μ-SPE have a significant influence on the efficient extraction of analytes [3]. To increase the enrichment and extraction capacity of adsorbents for target analytes, different types of µ-SPE sorbents have been used including, C18 sorbents [13], coconut husk fiber [14], Moringa oleifera seed [15], Fe3O4@SiO2 magnetic composite [16], melamine sponge anchoring with Ni/Al-LDH adsorbent [3] melamine sponge incorporated with carbon dots [17] and functionalized graphene oxide [18].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a novel carbon nanomaterial from the graphene family due to their unique physicochemical properties and diverse range of applications [19,20]. Due to their small size and huge specific surface area, they can offer a significant number of surface adsorption sites [21]. In addition, they also offer advantages such as facile synthesis, being nontoxic and having a lower manufacturing cost.

Corn stalk (CS) is an abundant byproduct in agriculture with obvious hydrophilicity [22] which can be divided into rind and pith. The CS pith (CSP) has a much lighter and three-dimensional honeycomb-like structure as the center part with various chemical groups on the surface [23]. To date, many studies have been conducted on the development of biosorbents using CS to adsorb single pollutants such as dyes and oils removal [22], heavy metals [24], and antibiotics [25]. To date, no studies on functional graphene carbon dots in corn stalk pith as a green sorbent have been reported.

Hence, the aim of the present research is to develop a functional graphene carbon dots in corn stalk pith as a low-cost sorbent for extraction of triazole residues before their quantitation by HPLC. The structural of as-prepared adsorbent was characterized by numerous techniques. This precursor has the characteristics of being inexpensive and renewable, two great advantages for obtaining fungicide adsorbents. Different factors affecting the extraction efficiency were evaluated by univariate experimental design. Additionally, the greenness and blueness assessment of the developed method was performed using the Analytical Eco-Scale (AEC) and AGREE tools to evaluate its environmental impact and analytical practicality. Finally, the validated method was successfully applied to determine triazole fungicide residues in different types of rice samples.

All triazole fungicides, including myclobutanil (MCBT), Triadimefon (TDF), tebuconazole (TBZ), hexaconazole (HCZ), and diniconazole (DCZ) were supplied by Dr. Ehren-storfer GmbH (Germany) and its purity was ≥99 %. Individual stock standard solutions (1000 mg L−1) were prepared by dissolving 10 mg of the pure compound in 10 mL methanol (HPLC grade, Merck, Darmstadt, Germany) and stored at 4 °C. When needed, the mixed standard solutions were diluted with deionized water into a series of working standard solutions. Graphite, n-hexane and acetonitrile (HPLC grade) were from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Citric acid monohydrate was purchased from KemAus (Australia). Deionized water was produced from a RiOs™ Type I Simplicity 185 system (Millipore water, USA) with a specific resistivity of 18.2 MΩ.cm.

A Waters 1525 Binary HPLC pump (Water, Massachusetts, USA) equipped with a diode array detector (Water, Massachusetts, USA) was employed for detection. Empower 3 software was used for data acquisition. The separation of all desired analytes was carried out with a C18 endcapped reverse-phase column (column specification: 150 × 4.6 mm, i.d. 5 µm, Purospher® STAR RP-18, Merck, Germany). Mobile phase used were acetonitrile and water in a ratio of 50:50 and the injection volume was 20 μL. The flow rate of the mobile phase was 1 mL min−1 and detection wavelength during chromatogram recording was chosen at 220 nm.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was used to scan the functional groups in the synthesized sorbent. The instrument used for FTIR measurements was Bruker Invenio-S FT-IR (Bruker corp, Massachusetts, USA) in the 4000–400 cm− 1 range. Scanning electron microscopy was used to examine the surface morphology of sponges. Scanning was performed using Field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) (SEM; TESCAN Model MIRA, UK). X-ray diffraction (XRD, Model D8 Advance, Bruker, USA was used for the determination of phase angle and crystallinity of the synthesized sorbent. TGA 4000 Thermogravimetric Analyzer (PerkinElmer, Inc., MA, USA) was used for analysis of thermal stabilities of the as-prepared sorbent. To identify the specific surface areas of porous material, Brunner-Emmett-Teller (BET) (Micromeritics® TriStar II Plus (Georgia, USA)) for determination of adsorption–desorption isotherms of N2 at 77 K. Hot air oven (UN55, Memmert, Germany) was used.
Vacuum manifold (Merck KGaA, Germany) and Millipore SigmaTM pump (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) were utilized for extraction method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of corn stalk pith and functional graphene carbon dots in corn stalk pith as a green sorbent
ce:section.ce:para: Corn stalk pith was first peeled and then rinsed multiple times with tab water followed by deionized water to remove the impurities. The materials were then cut into cylinder (diameter 1 cm and high 1 cm). Then, the as-prepared materials were dried at 60 °C until dryness (constant weight, approximately 3 h) and kept in a desiccator for further use.

The as-prepared corn stalk pith (1 piece) was then stirred in 10 mL of aqueous graphene carbon dots solution (0.1 g of citric acid, 0.25 g of urea and 0.1 g of graphite powder) for 1 h. The carbon dots solution was prepared following the previously reported method [26], with some modification. After that, the corn stalk shell was then dried overnight at ambient temperature. The corn stalk pith decorated with graphene carbon dots was then heated in the oven for 150 min at 130 °C to fully modify. Finally, the as-prepared biosorbent was stored in a desiccator until utilized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Micro-solid phase extraction (micro-SPE) using green investigated sorbent
ce:section.ce:para: An illustration of the micro-SPE procedure is shown in Fig. S1. The green investigated sorbent (1 piece) was put on cotton fabric in 5-mL syringe. The extraction columns were conditioned by 5 mL of hexane, followed by 5 mL of acetonitrile. After that, all the standard/sample solution (5 mL) mixed with 0.1 g of sodium acetate was passed through the cartridge at a flow rate of 2.0 mL min−1 using a 12-port SPE Vacuum Manifold coupled with Millipore SigmaTM pump. The analyte was adsorbed in the as-investigated sorbent and then desorbed by applying 400 µL of methanol on the column. The eluate was subjected to HPLC-PDA analysis.

The six rice samples under evaluation were procured from supermarkets located in the Maha Sarakham Province of Northeast Thailand. Rice samples were prepared following the previously reported method [27], with some modification. Each rice sample was grinded using a commercial grinder. After that, rice sample (0.5 g) was mixed with 3.00 mL of acetonitrile. before centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 3 min. The supernatant was dissolved with deionized water to the final volume of 5.00 mL, and filtered extraction using green investigated sorbent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the corn stalk pith decorated with graphene carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared corn stalk pith decorated with graphene carbon dots were characterized by FTIR, FE-SEM, BET, XRD, and TGA.

The FTIR spectra of corn stalk pith before and after modification with graphene carbon dots (Fig. 1 ) display characteristic absorption peaks corresponding to the functional groups of cellulose. Peaks at 3340, 2898, 1371, 1037, and 897 cm−1 are associated with cellulose [28]. Particularly, the 3340 cm−1 band may result from intramolecular hydrogen bonding (O–H stretching), while the 2898 cm−1 band is likely due to C–H stretching in CH and CH2 groups of cellulose compounds is linked to acetyl and ester groups of hemicelluloses or the ester linkage of lignin in cellulose. Peaks at 1658 and 1642 cm−1 likely correspond to the bending mode of adsorbed water. The 1371 cm−1 peak is associated with C–H bending and CH2 bending in carbohydrates, while the 1037 cm−1 peak corresponds to C–O–C pyranose ring skeletal vibration. The absence of the 897 cm−1 peak suggests that glycosidic linkages between sugar units were removed, indicating the complete removal of hemicelluloses and lignin after synthesis [29]. The cellulose structure underwent significant modification following pretreatment and modification of graphene carbon dots. After the carbonization process, a new FTIR band at 1658 cm−1, corresponding to C=C stretching vibrations, appeared, indicating that graphene carbon dots chemically interacted with cellulose, forming covalent bonds. The addition of graphene carbon dots had a dual impact, inducing both physical and chemical modifications in the corn stalk pith cellulose structure. Post-carbonization, the FTIR spectrum of the modified corn stalk pith (Fig. 1(f)) shows a peak near 1600 cm−1, attributed to aromatic C=C ring stretching, confirming the formation of conjugated structures. Differences in peak positions between unmodified and modified corn stalk pith result from oxidation, which disrupts intramolecular hydrogen bonds in cellulose and hemicellulose. This process leads to the formation of amorphous cellulose regions and alters the structural organization. The reduction in hydrogen bonding contributes to an increase in surface area and porosity, fundamentally changing the morphology and adsorption properties of the cellulose.

The microstructures of the corn stalk pith and corn stalk pith decorated with graphene carbon dots were investigated via FE-SEM. As is depicted in the Fig. 2 (a), The corn stalk pith exhibits a porous 3D web-like microstructure with a smooth surface and a porous framework, contributing to increased capacity and a substantial surface area. After the decoration of corn stalk pith with graphene carbon dots, all of the corn stalk pith had an interpenetrate porous structure (as shown in Fig. 2(b)). After adsorption and desorption, this results in a wrinkled and rough surface, creating a distinct morphology compared to without adsorption process.

N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms at 77 K was used for determination of the pore size of the corn stalk pith and corn stalk pith decorated with graphene carbon dots (as depicted in Fig. S2). BET analysis was conducted to investigate the surface area and pore volume of corn stalk pith, both in its unmodified (a) and modified (b) forms. This analysis was performed by measuring the volumes of nitrogen gas (N2) absorbed and desorbed on the corn stalk pith surfaces at the constant temperature of liquid nitrogen, with the results presented in Table 1 . For unmodified corn stalk pith, the BET surface area was determined to be 3.3741 m2/g, while the surface area decreased to 2.5551 m2/g following modification due to the pores of MeS was filled with carbon dot as a result decreased in the surface area and pore size [17]. The adsorption isotherm data indicates a Type-III isotherm, a characteristic of systems where the initial surface-sorbate interaction is weak, but sorbate molecules already present on the surface encourage additional sorbate molecules from the vapor phase to adsorb due to favorable sorbate–sorbate interactions. This isotherm type is marked by a rapid, exponential increase in adsorption at the beginning of increasing pressure or concentration, which then continues steadily until the relative pressure nears unity. These Type-III isotherm characteristics were observed for the interaction between the modified corn stalk pith and the target analytes [30,31]. Pore volume reflects the capacity of an adsorbent to absorb adsorbate, while pore diameter also plays a significant role in the adsorption process. Larger pore diameters generally correlate with higher pore volumes, thus enhancing the absorption capacity of the material. In this study, the pore volume and pore diameter were found in corn stalk pith with graphene carbon dots, with a pore volume of 0.001091 cm3/g and a pore diameter of 69.226 Å. This enhanced pore structure is attributed to the synthesis process involving corn stalk pith. During the modification process, graphene carbon dots were initially incorporated onto the corn stalk pith, establishing a framework for interaction with target analytes. This modification caused a portion of the original corn stalk pith pores to be filled with graphene carbon dots, resulting in a macroporous structure with a smaller pore size. As a consequence, both the overall surface area and pore size distribution were reduced in the modified material. This change in surface characteristics highlights how the addition of graphene carbon dots influences the adsorption properties of the corn stalk pith by altering its surface area and pore structure.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) technique was employed to assess the crystallinity of corn stalk pith before and after pre-treatment, as shown in Fig. 3 . Crystalline materials produce sharp, well-defined peaks, while amorphous materials exhibit broader, less distinct features [32]. The XRD pattern of raw corn stalk pith (b) indicated crystallinity, with prominent peaks at 2θ values of 21° and 26.5°. Diffraction patterns for different particle sizes of corn stalk pith showed a main peak around 22° and a minor peak near 10°, corresponding to the (002) and (101) planes of amorphous carbon, respectively [33]. Broader peaks observed in the graphene-decorated carbon dot samples suggest increased carbonization after graphene integration. Additionally, the diffractograms revealed that higher temperatures intensified the graphite peaks (a) and reduced the intensity of crystalline cellulose peaks, demonstrating that elevated temperatures favor the formation of crystalline graphite (c). Notably, the XRD patterns of corn stalks modified with carbon dots exhibited minimal shifts in peak positions, suggesting that the crystalline regions of the corn stalks pith were preserved during synthesis.

The thermal properties of materials are critical to their functionality in sorbent applications. Fig. 4 illustrates the thermal stability of corn stalk pith and graphene carbon dots in corn stalk pith, as determined by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). The TGA results revealed multiple stages of weight loss in the 30–900 °C range, corresponding to different thermal events. The initial weight loss up to 100 °C was attributed to moisture evaporation, a physical process due to the release of adsorbed water molecules. The onset of Torre faction, associated with the thermal degradation of organic compounds within the corn stalk pith, occurred around 220 °C. Complete thermal decomposition of the corn stalk pith was observed at approximately 410 °C, with further heating beyond 400 °C revealing specific thermal behavior, particularly at a heating rate of 10 °C/min. Three distinct stages of weight loss were identified during the thermal decomposition. The first stage, occurring below 100 °C, is primarily attributed to the loss of moisture from the biomass. In the second stage (primary decomposition), rapid devolatilization was observed between 200–400 °C, corresponding to approximately 50 % weight loss in the corn stalk pith. This stage reflects the thermal decomposition of the major biomass components: hemicellulose, cellulose, and lignin. Hemicellulose, the least thermally stable, decomposes between 200–350 °C, while cellulose decomposes in the range of 350–500 °C. Partial lignin decomposition, which occurs over a broader range, also begins in this temperature interval, as supported by Gani and Naruse [34]. The third stage (secondary decomposition) occurs between 400–900 °C, where significantly lower weight loss is observed. In this stage, the corn stalk pith exhibited around 10 % weight loss, indicating the decomposition of more stable organic fractions. Comparing the thermal behavior of corn stalk alone with that of corn stalk pith containing graphene carbon dots, it was evident that the presence of graphene carbon dots increased the thermal stability of the material. This improvement in thermal stability can be attributed to the enhanced thermal conductivity and interaction between the carbon dots and the biomass matrix, which delays further degradation. A similar stabilization effect was observed in studies involving graphene carbon dots synthesized under a nitrogen atmosphere. Since hemicellulose decomposes between 220–310 °C, the complete degradation of hemicellulose during pyrolysis likely contributed to the increased thermal resilience of the resulting material.

A one-variable-at-a-time optimization procedure for the composition of graphene carbon dot, temperature and time was performed. An aqueous sample (5 mL), at concentrations of 100 µg L−1 of triazoles, was assessed the extraction efficiency. Single corn stalk pith cubes (diameter 1 cm and high 1 cm) were used.

The citric acid is the only source of graphene carbon dot, its concentration can affect the percentage of analytes that could be extracted from the composite material. Different amount of citric acid (monohydrate) powder at 0.1, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75 and 1.0 g, were studied. The results were shown in Fig. S3(a). The results shown that the highest extraction efficiency of the studied triazoles ware obtained with the amount of citric acid 0.1 g. Thus, 0.1 g of citric acid was used for functional in corn stalk pith.

In order to investigate the efficacy of urea as precursor of graphene carbon dot for the detection of triazole in an aqueous sample, various amounts of urea (0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, and 0.75 g) were added. According to the results on Fig. S3(b), the extraction efficacy of the target triazole increased with increasing the amounts of urea up to 0.25 g, and then decreased. Therefore, the amount of urea 0.25 g was preferred for further study in this study.

The effect of graphite as precursor of graphene carbon dot was evaluated by varying the amount of graphite from 0.025 to 0.25 g. The results showed in Fig. S3(c) that the extraction efficiency of analytes increased with an increase in extraction time from 0.025 to 0.1 g, except DCZ. This result indicating that maximum extraction was obtained at 0.1 g of graphite.

The heating temperature was examined in the range of 90 and 130 °C, for 60 and 150 min. However, the melting point of anhydrous citric acid is 153 °C. At higher temperature than 130 °C, the porous of the corn stalk pith were broken. The heating temperature studied here was not higher than 130 °C to avoid burning the corn stalk pith. The results showed that as the temperature of the oven raised, the functional graphene carbon dots in corn stalk pith performed better considering that the extraction efficiency at 130 °C was higher than that obthain at lower temperature (as shown in Fig. S3(d)). As a result, the optimal temperature was found to be 130 °C. The heating time was also examined for 60 and 150 min and it was found that the performance of resulting the functional graphene carbon dots in corn stalk pith were shown in Fig. S3(e)). It was found that more than 150 min, the corn stalk pith was burn. Therefore, 150 min at 130 °C was selected due to shows highest response.

To obtain the highest extraction efficiency for extraction of the triazole fungicides, various factors were studied and optimized, including sample volume (mL), kind and amount of salt, and kind and volume of elution solvent. A one-variable-at-a-time optimization procedure were used. All experiments were examined in triplicate using standard solutions of triazoles 100 µg L−1. Relative standard deviation (%RSD) was utilized to assess experimental precision.

To obtain a high extraction efficiency of target -compounds at low concentration levels, sample volume was studied in the range of 5.0–15.0 mL. As shown in Fig. S4(a), the results show that the extraction of all triazole fungicides was satisfactory when the sample volume was 5.0 mL. After that, the sample volume above 5.0 mL, extraction efficiency significantly decreased due to the reduced contact between the increasing solution volume and the unchanged adsorbent surface area. Therefore, a sample volume of 5.0 mL was selected.

The addition of salt in the sample solution can increase the distribution constant of analytes and reduce their solubility, thus facilitating the extraction of the target analytes from the sample matrices [35]. In this study, different salts (NaCl, Na2SO4, CH3COONa and NH4Cl) at 0.1 g was added and the results were compared with that obtained from without salt addition. As can be seen in Fig. S4(b) indicated that the addition of CH3COONa was found to be adequate for the extraction the studied triazoles (as the highest peak area were obtained). Therefore, the addition of CH3COONa was selected for further studies. Therefore, different amounts of CH3COONa (0.05–0.5 g) were investigated on the extraction efficiency of the studied compounds (as shown in Fig. S4(c)). The result show that, the extraction efficiency of triazoles studied increased with the increase with increasing the amount of CH3COONa up to 0.1 g. After that, the extraction efficiency decreased. Therefore, subsequent experiments and analyses were conducted using 0.1 g of CH3COONa.

The sample pH is one of essential parameters for enhancing the extraction efficiency of the target analytes, which could affect the molecular and ionic state of the target analytes and as a result cause an increase or decrease in extraction efficiency of the target analytes. Moreover, the pH determines the charge on the surface of the adsorbents [36]. Thus, to obtain the highest extraction efficiency of the triazoles, the pH value from the range of 4–11 was investigated (as shown in Fig. S4(d)). As can be seen, with increasing the pH value from 4 to 7 and then slightly decreased. These due to the concentration of hydroxyl ions in the solution increased when the pH value more than 7. In other words, when the hydroxyl group present in the environment reacts with the active groups of the absorbent and the target analytes, it causes water to escape and create negative charges in both the adsorbent and the target analytes [37]. When both the adsorbent and the target analytes have the same charges, as a result, due to the electrostatic repulsion created between the synthesized adsorbent and the target analytes, the extraction efficiency decreases [36]. Therefore, pH = 7 was selected as the optimal pH for further study.

The selection of a suitable solvent, which are an essential parameter for micro-SPE, must be compatible with the analytical instrument in this study, namely, the HPLC-DAD. Because of these reasons, several polar solvents were tested, including acetonitrile, methanol, and ethanol (as shown in Fig. S4(e)). It was found that employing methanol contributed to higher extraction efficiency thanks to similar polarity to analytes. Thus, methanol was selected as the elution solvent for further studied. The effect of volume of the methanol (as elution solvent) was also studied in the range of 100–5000 µL. As shown in Fig. S4(f), the best extraction efficiencies of all analytes were obtained with 400.0 μL. Employing smaller volumes resulted in poor reproducibility and incomplete desorption due to the lack of full sorbent coverage whilst volumes exceeding 400.0 μL brought about unsatisfactory extraction yield because of dilution. Therefore, volume of the methanol of 400 µL was chosen.

The potential recyclability and regenerating ability of the as-prepared sorbent was assessed by conducting the reusability test (as shown in Fig. S4(g)). The repeated use of the adsorbent was occurred after washed with methanol and then dried. It was found that the degree of sorption of the adsorbent after 3 cycles had significant decreased, indicating that the as-prepared sorbent could be reused at least 3 times. After that, the extraction efficiency decreased due to the functional graphene carbon dots in corn stalk pith was destroyed. As a result, the as-prepared sorbent could be reused 3 cycles.

Through selectivity exploration, the feasibility of as-prepared sorbent for detecting triazoles was firstly assessed. The extraction performance of the investigated material of carbamate pesticides (methomyl, carbofuran, carbaryl, isoprocarb, fenobucarb), and neonicotinoid insecticides (clothianidin, imidacloprid, dinotefuran, thiacloprid) were evaluated. Rice samples were selected as a model. It was found that that only studied triazoles were extracted the as-prepared material the studied samples. Therefore, the selectivity of the as-prepared material higher than some generally utilized insecticides.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The adsorption mechanism of graphene carbon dots in corn stalk pith
ce:section.ce:para: Citric acid and urea were used as the starting substances to form carbon dots by a thermal decomposition reaction. During heating around 130 °C, citric acid decomposes to form carbonaceous structures, while urea contributes nitrogen doping from its amine and carbonyl groups. The reaction (as shown in Fig. 5 ) results in highly nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with functional groups such as carboxyl (–COOH), hydroxyl (–OH), and amine (–NH2) [38]. Therefore, graphite was added to create an interaction between CDs and graphite to increase the stability of the carbon dot particles. Graphite has a layered sp2-carbon structure, which allows for π-π interactions with the aromatic domains of the N-CDs. The carboxyl (–COOH) and amine (–NH2) groups on CDs can interact with graphite via hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions. This attachment enhances the dispersion of CDs on graphite and improves conductivity, which is useful for electronic and catalytic applications [39]. Because the entry of CDs between the graphite layers caused a weak force, the solution was coated on the corn stalk pith again to increase the stability of the adsorbent. Corn stalk pith is highly porous and contains cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin, which have hydroxyl (–OH) and carboxyl (–COOH) functional groups. The N-CD/graphite composite can adhere to corn stalk pith through hydrogen bonding between hydroxyl groups in corn stalk pith and the carboxyl or amino groups of CDs. Electrostatic attraction if there are surface charges on corn stalk pith. Van der Waals interactions between corn stalk pith and graphite.

The mechanism that is expected to occur is (i.) adsorption on urea-CDs/graphite-coated corn stalk pith. The CDs/graphite composite coated on corn stalk pith provides a large surface area for fungicide adsorption. The hydroxyl (–OH) and carboxyl (–COOH) groups on CDs and corn stalk pith interact with the nitrogen atoms in the triazole ring via hydrogen bonding. The porous structure of corn stalk pith helps in the physical adsorption and entrapment of triazole fungicides. And (ii.) π-π stacking with graphite and CDs, the triazole ring and aromatic backbone of fungicides interact via π-π stacking with the sp2-hybridized carbon structures of graphite and CDs. This interaction stabilizes the adsorption and helps in capturing the fungicides effectively. And (iii.) electrostatic interactions depending on the pH of the solution, the carboxyl (–COOH) and amine (–NH2) groups on CDs can be protonated or deprotonated, affecting their charge. Triazole fungicides often have polar functional groups, leading to electrostatic attraction or repulsion with the functionalized CD/graphite/corn stalk pith material. And (iv.) hydrogen bonding, the hydrogen donor–acceptor interactions between triazole nitrogen atoms and hydroxyl/carboxyl groups in CDs and corn stalk pith contribute to stronger adsorption. If the triazole fungicide contains fluorine or hydroxyl groups, it may further enhance hydrogen bonding with corn stalk pith or CDs [40,41]. Therefore, the aforementioned mechanisms can confirm the occurrence of the described process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical figures of merit of the studied method
ce:section.ce:para: The method displayed satisfactory results under the optimum extraction conditions detection of triazole fungicides, the analytical characteristics of the method were studied such as linear ranges, determination coefficient (R2), limits of detections (LODs), and relative standard deviations (RSDs) of test repeatability are listed in Table 2 . Calibration plot was drawn for each analyte concentration on the x-axis and corresponding peak area on the y-axis. The evaluation of linearity was carried out through determination coefficients (R2) and the values were always higher than 0.98 (as shown in Table 2) for all target analytes. The signal values of LOD or LOQ concentrations were calculated as three or ten times the standard deviation of signal values of the blank samples [42]. The LODs and LOQs were 0.03 μg L−1 and 0.09 μg L−1, respectively. The intra-day precision was calculated by selecting five time points within 24 h, the RSD ranged from 0.01–0.09 % and 0.64–4.68 %, for retention time (tR) and peak area, respectively. The inter-day precision was calculated once daily for five consecutive days, and the RSD ranged from 0.08–0.13 % and 2.6–6.0 %, for retention time (tR) and peak area, respectively.

Enrichment factors (EFs), defined as the ratio of the slope of regression equations with and without the application of the proposed method, were found to be in the range of 3.17–11.89 folds. Representative HPLC chromatograms of direct analysis and after extraction by the investigated sorbent method were demonstrated in Fig. S5.

To verify the practicability of the developed method, the corn stalk pith decorated with graphene carbon dots sorbent was utilized to extract rice samples. A matrix-matched calibration approach was used to investigate the matrix effect in real samples (as shown in Table S1). Matrix-matched calibration curves was prepared by spiked standard triazoles in the range of 90–500 μg kg−1 of each target analyte into rice samples and then extracted by the proposed method. Excellent linearity of all triazoles in rice samples with R2 higher than 0.99, were obtained. Triazole fungicides were not detected in any of the real samples or otherwise were below the detection limits.

The ME, when matrix-matched calibration was used, is expressed as the Eq. (1) [43]. The ME was calculated from the slope of the calibration curves the different matrices used [44] and evaluated according to the established range of low (<20 %), moderate (20–50 %), and high (>50 %) [45].
(1)
M E
( % )
=
Slope
of
calibration
curve
in
matrix
Slope
of
calibration
curve
in
solvent
-
1
×
100

Matrix effect values are presented in Table S2. In all sample extracts, the analytes exhibited matrix effects smaller than 20 % (MCBT in rice II, rice III, rice V, rice VI; TDF in rice II, rice IV, rice V, rice VI; TBZ in rice I, rice IV, rice V; HCZ in rice I, rice I, rice III, rice IV, rice VI; DCZ in rice III, rice VI), moderated matrix effect in the range of 20–50 % (MCBT in rice I, rice IV; TDF in rice I, rice III; TBZ in rice II, rice III, rice VI; HCZ in rice V; DCZ in rice I, rice II, rice IV, rice V) and high matrix effect (TDF in rice I). The accuracy of the developed method was evaluated by spiking the known concentrations of each analyte at three different levels (90, 300, and 500 µg kg−1) to the real samples. The results show that the corresponding recoveries of the spiked rice samples were in the range of 59.46–139.20 %. The obtained results were shown in Table S3. It was found that no residue of the studied triazoles was observed in rice sample studies. The typical chromatograms of studied rice sample are demonstrated in Fig. S6.
Two method greenness were evaluated, including Analytical Eco-Scale (AEC) procedure [46] and Analytical GREEnness metric (AGREE) [47].

The ideal green analysis of the Analytical Eco-Scale has a value of 100. Each parameter of the analytical method was used for evaluation of penalty points (PP) that deviate from the ideal green analysis (reagent use, risk, energy and waste). The numerical value of the Analytical Eco-Scale was calculated from the sum of the penalty points subtracted from 100 (AEC = 100 − total PPs). The methods with scores higher than 75 indicate an excellent solvent. Those with green scores in the range 75 < x < 50 are acceptable, and values below 50 indicate inadequate green characters. Thus, the greenness score attributed to the developed method is 65, which confirms it is acceptable from greenness. The calculations are given in Table 3 .

AGREE is a green metric system which evaluates adherence to the 12 principles of Green Analytical Chemistry [47]. For each principle, the algorithm evaluates the performance of the proposed analytical method by assigning scores from 0 to 1 for each criterion. As depicted in Table 3, the inestigated method can be acceptable as a green due to a final score the AGREE evaluation of 0.48. Device positioning (in Sector 3) and source of reagents (in Sector 10) showed the lowest performing subsectors in AGREE. The evaluation, assessed by Analytical Eco-scale and Analytical GREEnness metric (AGREE), confirmed its acceptable as a green method.

The developed method was compared with previous applications to determine triazole as shown in Table 4
[15,17,48–51]. The important point of this method is introducing an alternative biosorbent which is based on the corn stalk pith decorated with graphene carbon dots, easy and quick operation. The detection limit of the investigated method is almost comparable to that obtained from other microextraction method. The presented method achieves low detection limit, which are below the MRLs established by Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC) for triazole fungicides. This proposed can be used as a powerful alternative miniaturized extraction and preconcentration method for the analysis of triazole fungicides in various samples.

This study describes the functional graphene carbon dots in corn stalk pith via a one-step method and used to prepare for the extraction of triazole fungicides prior HPLC analysis. The as-prepared showed outstanding inter-day and intraday precision, wide linear range, low limits of detection and high recoveries for the determination of triazole fungicides from rice samples. This alternative sorbent was exhibited some selectivity over some commonly used pesticides and cost-effective for the determination of triazoles. The low cost of the materials needed for the preparation of functional graphene carbon dots in corn stalk pith (i.e., corn stalk pith sponge and citric acid) combined with the simplicity of the preparation, (requires only 150 min of heating), makes it ideal for the production on a larger scale. This study not only proposed a method for analysis of carbamates from rice samples, but also explored the potential of corn stalk pith in adsorption of organic pollutants.

Woranut Silaram: Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Rossukhon Boonlerd: Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Phatchara Rattanaphonsaen: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Data curation, Conceptualization. Wannipha Khiaophong: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Data curation, Conceptualization. Rawikan Kachangoon: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Data curation, Conceptualization. Norio Teshima: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Conceptualization. Kansiri Pakkethati: Visualization, Data curation, Conceptualization. Siriboon Mukdasai: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Conceptualization. Kraingkrai Ponhong: Visualization, Conceptualization. Jitlada Vichapong: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research project was financially supported by Thailand Science Research and Innovation (TSRI) and Faculty of Science, Mahasarakham University, Thailand.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.113087.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
